title: It Takes a Brit
summary: "We look back on this period in a sort of shocked wonder at how such a system—slavery—could have established itself in our country, except that it was a sign of the times and that others practiced it, including France, Britain, and the Arabs. But Britain and then France outlawed it well before the American Civil War."
body: It takes a Brit, perhaps, to give us an unvarnished view of the evils of slavery in the United States, known throughout the antebellum South as "the peculiar institution." (I am not counting the uproariously entertaining Django Unchained of Quentin Tarantino, which matched the levels of preposterousness of his previous film, Inglorious Basterds .) There are very few heroes in British Director Steve McQueen's 12 Years a Slave , a fact-based account of the abduction of a free Negro, Solomon Northup, in Washington, D.C., and his forcible deportation to Louisiana in the 1840s. There is the first plantation owner, played by Benedict Cumberbatch, who helps the violin playing Solomon (in a beautifully nuanced performance by Chiwetel Ejiofor), and a liberal-minded Canadian, played by Brad Pitt, who turns to the law to achieve the release of the protagonist. Otherwise, in this masterful (and long) film, the white oppressors, from the second plantation owner (Michael Fassbender) on down, display an unremitting cruelty, separating a mother from her children, flogging a teenage black girl, and hanging from a tree two black youths, apparent runaways, etc., etc. We look back on this period in a sort of shocked wonder at how such a system—slavery—could have established itself in our country, except that it was a sign of the times and that others practiced it, including France, Britain, and the Arabs. But Britain and then France outlawed it well before the American Civil War. In recent days, there has been a demand from a group of Caribbean nations for reparations from the former colonial powers in the region. Such a demand, in my view, is invalid in the case of the United States, where the "reparations" have been paid in the blood of Union soldiers. Statements and views expressed in this commentary are solely those of the author and do not imply endorsement by Harvard University, the Harvard Kennedy School, or the Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs. The latest from Belfer See more Recommended citation Cogan, Charles. "It Takes a Brit." Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School, November 7, 2013. result.main_content_en: The article discusses the film '12 Years a Slave' by British director Steve McQueen, which offers a stark portrayal of slavery in the antebellum United States. It contrasts the film's unflinching depiction of cruelty with the more fantastical approach of other works. The narrative highlights the historical context of slavery as a widespread practice, noting that Britain and France abolished it before the American Civil War. The piece concludes by addressing contemporary calls for reparations, arguing that in the U.S. context, the bloodshed of the Civil War constituted a form of reparation, thereby dismissing current claims. The views expressed are attributed solely to the author.
result.main_content_zh: 本文围绕英国导演史蒂夫·麦奎因的电影《为奴十二年》展开，该片对美国南北战争前的奴隶制进行了赤裸而残酷的描绘。文章将这部电影与其他更具幻想色彩的作品进行对比，强调了片中白人压迫者持续的残忍行径。作者指出奴隶制是当时时代的标志，并非美国独有，但英国和法国在美国内战前就已废除奴隶制。文章最后提及近期加勒比国家向原殖民宗主国索求赔偿的呼声，但认为美国的“赔偿”已通过内战期间联邦士兵的鲜血支付，因此驳回了相关的赔偿要求。文中观点仅代表作者个人立场。
